title: Why LLMs Hallucinate
concept: The model has no internal concept of truth; it only outputs the statistically most likely token sequence based on its training data patterns.

exercises.prompt: Does a Large Language Model (LLM) know when it is telling the truth?

options.text: An LLM knows what is true but chooses to lie.
An LLM has no internal concept of truth; it just predicts likely words.
An LLM connects to a live human mind to find the truth.

exercises.explain: An LLM has no concept of what is actually true. It only knows what words are statistically likely to go together based on its training.

cards.title: When AI Makes Things Up
cards.body: Have you ever seen an AI confidently tell you a fact that was completely, hilariously wrong? It wasn't trying to trick you! Let's find out why these brilliant machines can make such silly mistakes.

cards.title: What is a Hallucination?
cards.body: A 'hallucination' happens when an AI model confidently generates convincing text that is completely incorrect or made up. This happens because the model does not actually know what is 'true' or 'false'—it only knows what sounds grammatically and statistically correct based on its training patterns.

cards.title: The Super Autocomplete
cards.body: Think of an LLM like an autocomplete feature on your phone, but on steroids. When you type 'Happy...', your phone might suggest 'Birthday' because it has seen those two words together millions of times. Your phone doesn't actually know if it is your birthday; it is just playing a high-speed game of statistical guessing!

cards.title: Creating Fake Bios
cards.body: If you ask an AI to write a biography of a minor historical figure, it might confidently list a realistic-looking birth date, college, and list of achievements. While they sound perfectly plausible, these details are often completely made up because the model filled the blanks with common patterns!

cards.title: Myth: AI Lies on Purpose
cards.body: People often think that when an AI hallucinates, it is actively lying or has a virus. In reality, the AI is doing exactly what it was designed to do: predicting the next most likely word! It just doesn't have a way to verify if its predictions match actual reality.

cards.title: Verification vs. Probability
cards.body: Let's look at how we verify facts versus how an LLM generates text. Notice how the AI focuses on patterns rather than checking facts!

left.text: Looks up reliable sources, checks historical records, and verifies truth.

right.text: Calculates the mathematical probability of words appearing together based on training data.

exercises.prompt: How do LLMs decide which word to output next?

options.text: They check a built-in encyclopedia of verified truths before typing.
They predict the most likely next word based on patterns they have seen.
They ask human moderators for approval in real-time.
They copy and paste directly from a secret database of correct answers.

exercises.explain: An LLM generates text by calculating which word is statistically most likely to follow the current text, based on patterns in its training data. It does not check a database of truth.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LLMs have an internal mechanism that verifies whether a statement is factually true before outputting it.
exercises.explain: LLMs do not have an internal database of truth or a system to verify facts. They only calculate statistical probabilities.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to explain how an AI chooses the next word in a sentence:
exercises.sentence: Instead of checking if a fact is true, an LLM simply outputs the _________ most likely sequence of words.
exercises.explain: AI models use probability, not factual truth, to decide which word comes next. They choose the statistically most likely word based on patterns in their training data.

exercises.prompt: Which of these responses is a clear example of an AI hallucination?

options.text: 'Water is made of hydrogen and oxygen atoms.'
'The famous Treaty of Sparkville was signed in 1994 by three sitting kings.'

exercises.explain: Option B is a hallucination because the AI confidently fabricated a nonexistent historical event by combining common words ('Treaty', '1994') into a plausible-sounding sentence.

exercises.prompt: Why do AI models hallucinate and confidently state incorrect information?

options.text: They are programmed to intentionally lie when they run out of memory.
They only predict statistically likely words and have no concept of actual truth.
They look up facts in a real-time web dictionary for every single word.
They temporarily lose connection to their main database of real facts.

exercises.explain: Large language models do not have an internal database of 'real-world facts' or a sense of 'truth.' They operate purely by calculating which word is statistically most likely to follow the preceding text based on their training data. When that statistical prediction doesn't line up with reality, we get a hallucination.
